source: CIS AWS COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.1.0
title: Ensure that HTTPS is enabled on load balancer

input_row.source: CIS AWS COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.1.0
input_row.title: Ensure that HTTPS is enabled on load balancer
input_row.description: The simplest way to use HTTPS with an Elastic Beanstalk environment is to assign a server certificate to your environment's load balancer.
input_row.rationale: When you configure your load balancer to terminate HTTPS, the connection between the client and the load balancer is secure.
input_row.audit: From the Console: 1. Login to AWS Console using https://console.aws.amazon.com/elasticbeanstalk 2. On the left hand side click Environments 3. Click on the Environment name that you want to review 4. Under the "environment_name-env" in the left column click Configuration 5. Scroll down under Configurations 6. Under category look for Load balancer 7. Click Edit 8. Under the Listeners section 9. Check the Listeners section for any enabled listeners and make sure the Protocol is set to HTTPS and Enabled. 10. If the Listener is required for HTTP and is not set to HTTPS refer to the remediation below. 11. Repeat steps 3-10 for each environment within the current region. 12. Then repeat the Audit process for all other regions.
input_row.remediation: From the Console: 1. Login to AWS Console using https://console.aws.amazon.com/elasticbeanstalk 2. On the left hand side click Environments 3. Click on the Environment name that you want to review 4. Under the "environment_name-env" in the left column click Configuration 5. Scroll down under Configurations 6. Under category look for Load balancer 7. Click Edit 8. Under the Listeners section 9. Click Add listener Set listener port Set Listener protocol to HTTPS Set Instance Port Sent Instance protocol to HTTPS Select your SSL certificate 10. Click Add 11. Make sure it is listed as enabled. If you have other listeners not using HTTPS make sure to turn off enabled 12. Click Apply to save the configuration changes. 13. Repeat steps 3-12 for each environment within the current region. 14. Then repeat the remediation for all other regions. References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticbeanstalk/latest/dg/configuring-https.html

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

The compliance control "Ensure that HTTPS is enabled on load balancer" involves checking the listener configurations of load balancers for Elastic Beanstalk environments to ensure that HTTPS is used as the protocol. This task can be automated by utilizing AWS APIs to retrieve and inspect the configuration of the load balancers.

AWS offers APIs and SDKs (such as boto3) to interact with Elastic Beanstalk and the underlying Elastic Load Balancing services. Using these APIs, it is possible to programmatically access the configurations of the load balancers, specifically to list their listeners and check if HTTPS is enabled.

Given the criteria:
- AWS APIs can query the properties related to load balancer listeners.
- The load balancer and its listener configurations are standard AWS service properties, accessible programmatically.
- boto3 SDK and AWS CLI have methods/commands to retrieve these configurations, particularly the load balancer listeners.
- The process involves querying configuration data available in AWS, not requiring interaction with application-level or OS-level settings.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_elb_load_balancer_https_listener_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

To automate the audit of ensuring that HTTPS is enabled on load balancers for Elastic Beanstalk environments:

1. **Enumerate Elastic Beanstalk Environments:** Use `boto3` to list all Elastic Beanstalk environments across the AWS account and regions.

2. **Get Load Balancer Details:**
   - Use the `describe_environments` method from the Elastic Beanstalk client to get details for each environment.
   - Retrieve the associated load balancer name(s) from the environment resources.

3. **Check Load Balancer Listeners:**
   - Use the Elastic Load Balancing client (`boto3.client('elbv2')` for Application Load Balancers or `boto3.client('elb')` for Classic Load Balancers).
   - Use the `describe_load_balancers` and `describe_listeners` methods to list the listeners for each load balancer.

4. **Verify HTTPS Protocol:**
   - For each listener retrieved, check if the protocol is set to HTTPS.
   - Confirm that each HTTPS listener also has a valid SSL certificate attached.

5. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - Pass: The load balancer has at least one listener with the HTTPS protocol enabled.
   - Fail: No HTTPS listener is present, or HTTP listeners are still enabled without a corresponding HTTPS listener.

This approach enables an automated, scalable solution to ensure compliance with the control across multiple Elastic Beanstalk environments and load balancers in various regions.
